articles.title: Quarantine the malformed traffic source before tuning the parser
articles.summary: Start by isolating the bad sender when malformed SIP comes from a known generator or peer.
content: When to use: INVITE or REGISTER validation errors are concentrated around one user agent, lab source, or ingress peer.
Action: Rate-limit, isolate, or block the offending sender while preserving clean traffic from trusted sources.
Why it works: Removing the bad source lowers immediate noise and protects healthy SIP flows while deeper analysis continues.
Verify: Parser rejects fall sharply and remaining traffic shows the expected header and body structure.
Operator hint: Save a representative malformed payload so later RCA output can reference the exact defect class.

articles.title: Enforce SIPp payload validation in synthetic scenarios
articles.summary: Bad scenario templates can repeatedly create malformed requests that look like production parser issues.
content: When to use: Validation failures began after a scenario update or a new SIPp payload template was introduced.
Action: Compare the current generator template to a known-good message, then fix header order, required fields, and content formatting.
Why it works: Correcting the source template removes the malformed wave without touching stable platform controls.
Verify: Newly generated requests pass schema checks and the reject pattern disappears from the same scenario.
Documentation: Tag the broken template revision in the incident so future knowledge retrieval can rank it quickly.

articles.title: Normalize Content-Length and SDP formatting on malformed INVITE paths
articles.summary: Content length, line ending, and SDP shape errors are common causes of 4xx validation bursts.
content: When to use: INVITE requests reach the parser but fail during body validation or SDP parsing.
Action: Verify Content-Length, line endings, MIME boundaries, and SDP section integrity against a known-good sample.
Why it works: Many malformed SIP incidents come from body formatting issues rather than missing top-level headers.
Verify: The same payload family begins passing validation and 4xx response concentration drops for INVITE traffic.
Guardrail: Fix the source message first rather than relaxing validation rules globally.

articles.title: Inspect SBC or edge header rewrites that are corrupting messages
articles.summary: A rewrite rule can turn valid messages into malformed traffic after they leave the source.
content: When to use: The source claims to send valid SIP, but captures at the validator show missing or mutated headers.
Action: Audit the rewrite rules on the edge proxy or SBC and compare ingress and egress copies of the same message.
Why it works: Catching the transformation point prevents teams from blaming the wrong system and broadening the incident unnecessarily.
Verify: The corrupted header pattern disappears after the rewrite rule is fixed or bypassed.
Evidence: Attach both pre-rewrite and post-rewrite samples to the incident article set.

articles.title: Tighten parser observability so malformed classes are obvious
articles.summary: Granular parser errors help separate malformed headers, bad bodies, and policy rejects.
content: When to use: Operators can see a 4xx burst but cannot tell whether the validator failed on syntax, policy, or body content.
Action: Expose error labels or counters that bucket malformed traffic into header, body, schema, and policy categories.
Why it works: Better observability shortens RCA time and prevents over-broad mitigations like disabling validation.
Verify: The incident dashboard shows a dominant malformed class instead of one opaque error bucket.
Follow-up: Use the top malformed class as a retrieval hint for future knowledge articles.

articles.title: Reject oversized SIP bodies at the edge instead of burdening core validators
articles.summary: Huge malformed bodies can consume parser resources before they are safely rejected.
content: When to use: Malformed SIP includes abnormally large bodies or attachments that inflate parsing latency.
Action: Apply sane size limits at the edge and ensure oversized traffic is rejected before it reaches the central validation path.
Why it works: Early rejection protects parser capacity for well-formed traffic and reduces latency amplification.
Verify: Validation latency drops and oversized requests are rejected consistently at the ingress boundary.
Risk: Confirm the size ceiling still allows legitimate use cases such as standard SDP offers.

articles.title: Verify codec declarations and media lines in malformed session offers
articles.summary: Malformed SDP often hides in codec lists or inconsistent media descriptions.
content: When to use: Calls fail during INVITE processing after the validator reaches the SDP section.
Action: Check media ports, codec declarations, payload numbers, and required SDP line order against a passing call sample.
Why it works: SDP structure issues can look like generic SIP validation failures until the offer-answer details are reviewed.
Verify: The corrected offer passes validation and session setup moves past the previous failure point.
Note: Keep one good and one bad SDP example linked to the same incident for future similarity search.

articles.title: Rollback a bad message-template change when malformed traffic started after deployment
articles.summary: If malformed SIP began right after a rollout, stabilize first by restoring the previous template.
content: When to use: A deployment changed message generation or normalization logic and malformed SIP started immediately afterward.
Action: Restore the last known-good template or formatter revision, then compare the diffs offline.
Why it works: Validation incidents caused by template regressions are often eliminated faster by rollback than by live patching.
Verify: The malformed signature disappears on the prior revision and healthy traffic resumes normal success rates.
Aftercare: Add a regression sample to pre-release validation so the same template bug cannot recur unnoticed.

articles.title: Block or degrade traffic from an untrusted user agent fingerprint
articles.summary: A single bad client build can flood the validator with malformed messages.
content: When to use: Malformed requests all share the same User-Agent, source range, or peer signature.
Action: Apply a temporary block, quarantine policy, or low-rate lane for that fingerprint while investigating the source build.
Why it works: Fingerprint-based isolation protects the rest of the platform without relaxing core SIP validation.
Verify: The dominant malformed signature disappears and the remaining traffic has a normal parser success rate.
Escalate: Work with the sender owner to fix the client build before re-enabling normal traffic.

articles.title: Compare one passing INVITE and one failing INVITE side by side
articles.summary: Side-by-side comparison is often the fastest way to isolate the exact malformed field.
content: When to use: Generic logs say the request is malformed, but operators cannot see which field actually differs.
Action: Compare one known-good message and one failing message field by field, including headers, ordering, and body formatting.
Why it works: The diff often reveals a single missing header, invalid delimiter, or body mismatch that broad monitoring cannot show.
Verify: The corrected message shape passes validation in the same path where it previously failed.
Operator tip: Preserve the sanitized diff as a reusable knowledge artifact for future incidents.
